title: Optimal Color Arrangement under Frequency Constraints

statement.markdown: You are given two arrays, `c` and `freq`, representing the colors and frequencies of a set of paint cans, respectively. The goal is to arrange the paint cans in such a way that every color is used at most once and the total frequency of each color group is as close to `freq[i]` as possible. The input arrays will always be in lexicographical order.
statement.inputFormat: A 1D array of integers `c` representing the colors and a 1D array of integers `freq` representing the frequencies.
statement.outputFormat: A 1D array of integers representing the optimal arrangement of the paint cans.
statement.notes: The output array should be in lexicographical order.

examples.explanation: For each color group, the frequency of the colors is as close to the target frequency as possible.

hints.text: Use a binary indexed tree to efficiently track the frequency of each color.

editorial.title: Optimal Color Arrangement under Frequency Constraints
editorial.intuition: The problem is a classic example of a greedy algorithm problem. We can solve it by sorting the input arrays and then iteratively assigning colors to each color group.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve considering all possible arrangements and calculating their total frequency for each color group.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a binary indexed tree to efficiently track the frequency of each color, allowing us to make changes to the arrangement in O(log n) time.
editorial.optimalSolution: The time complexity of the optimal solution is O(n log n) due to the sorting step.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven using a proof by contradiction.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly updating the frequency of each color in the binary indexed tree.
Not considering the edge cases where the color groups have different sizes.

functionSignature.cpp: void solve(vector<int>& c, vector<int>& freq, vector<int>& result);
functionSignature.java: public void solve(int[] c, int[] freq, int[] result);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, c: list[int], freq: list[int], result: list[int]):
